proposed_hypotheses.text: At least some of the 122 anonymized features (`feature_001`...`feature_123`) are non-null univariate predictors of `pfs_months`; specifically, the strongest signals will identify a small set of features (continuous and binary) with very small p-values relative to the rest.

analyses.result_summary: Universal screen across all 122 features. Strongest associations (rank-ordered by p): feature_078 (continuous, Pearson r=+0.766, p<1e-300), feature_057 (3-level, ANOVA F=3528, p<1e-300; means 5.27/4.05/2.91), feature_051 (binary, ANOVA F=4708, p<1e-300; mean PFS 5.05 vs 3.70), feature_038 (binary, F=1483, p<1e-300; mean 5.09 vs 4.12), feature_099 (continuous, r=-0.123, p=2e-167), feature_092 (continuous, r=+0.102, p=3e-115), feature_013 (binary, p=9e-56), feature_043 (binary, p=5e-46), feature_009 (continuous, r=-0.038, p=3e-17), feature_109 (binary, p=4e-6), feature_067 (binary, p=2e-4). The remaining ~100 features had p>=0.01 and effect sizes below clinical relevance.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_078` (continuous, range 30-90 with mean ~65, plausibly age in years) is positively associated with `pfs_months`: each one-unit increase in `feature_078` is associated with longer PFS on the natural scale.
Mean `pfs_months` increases monotonically across quartiles of `feature_078`, indicating an approximately linear (not just monotonic) dose-response relationship.

analyses.result_summary: Simple linear regression: slope = +0.1765 months PFS per unit increase in feature_078, p<1e-300, R^2=0.587. Each year (or unit) corresponds to ~0.18 months longer PFS; over the 30-90 observed range that implies ~10.6 months of swing.

Mean PFS by quartile of feature_078: Q1=2.09, Q2=3.71, Q3=4.90, Q4=6.59 months. Strictly monotonic increase across quartiles, with roughly linear spacing (deltas ~1.6, 1.2, 1.7) consistent with a near-linear relationship.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_057` (three-level integer 0/1/2) shows a monotonic decrease in mean `pfs_months` from level 0 → 1 → 2, consistent with an ordinal severity/stage indicator where higher values mean worse prognosis.
Treating `feature_057` as a continuous covariate (linear trend) the slope is significantly negative.

analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS by feature_057 level: 0 → 5.27 mo (n=17,592); 1 → 4.05 mo (n=24,971); 2 → 2.91 mo (n=7,437). Strictly monotonic decrease. Overall ANOVA F-test p<1e-300. Pairwise differences: level 1 vs 0 = -1.22 mo; level 2 vs 0 = -2.36 mo.
Linear-trend regression on feature_057: slope = -1.190 months per level increase, p<1e-300. Strong, monotonic, near-linear relationship.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_051`=1 is associated with shorter `pfs_months` than feature_051=0 (poor-prognostic biomarker).
`feature_038`=1 is associated with longer `pfs_months` than feature_038=0 (likely a beneficial treatment or favorable biomarker).
`feature_013`=1 is associated with shorter `pfs_months` than feature_013=0.
`feature_043`=1 is associated with shorter `pfs_months` than feature_043=0.
`feature_109`=1 is associated with shorter `pfs_months` than feature_109=0.
`feature_067`=1 is associated with longer `pfs_months` than feature_067=0.

analyses.result_summary: feature_051 prevalence 55%. Mean PFS: 3.70 (1) vs 5.05 (0); diff = -1.352 months, Welch t-test p<1e-300.
feature_038 prevalence 20%. Mean PFS: 5.09 (1) vs 4.12 (0); diff = +0.972 months, Welch p=1.7e-216.
feature_013 prevalence 42%. Mean PFS: 4.12 (1) vs 4.45 (0); diff = -0.327 months, Welch p=1.9e-58.
feature_043 prevalence 35%. Mean PFS: 4.11 (1) vs 4.42 (0); diff = -0.305 months, Welch p=1.0e-49.
feature_109 prevalence 4.5% (n=2272). Mean PFS: 4.09 (1) vs 4.32 (0); diff = -0.228 months, p=3.5e-7.
feature_067 prevalence 3% (n=1506). Mean PFS: 4.53 (1) vs 4.30 (0); diff = +0.221 months, p=7.3e-4.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_099` (continuous, range 0-25, right-skewed with median 3) is negatively associated with `pfs_months` even after adjusting for `feature_078`.
`feature_092` (continuous, range 1.5-5.5, fairly symmetric) is positively associated with `pfs_months` after adjusting for `feature_078`.
`feature_009` (continuous, right-skewed up to 778) is negatively associated with `pfs_months` after adjusting for `feature_078`.

analyses.result_summary: Adjusted slope of feature_099 = -0.0747 mo per unit, p<1e-300. Negative association persists and is in fact stronger relative to its standard error after age adjustment than unadjusted (raw r=-0.123).

Adjusted slope of feature_092 = +0.454 mo per unit, p=1.8e-264. Substantially LARGER than the unadjusted r=+0.10 — feature_078 was a negative confounder; with it controlled, feature_092 emerges as an important positive predictor.
Adjusted slope of feature_009 = -0.00466 mo per unit (raw scale, where the unit is small relative to the 0-778 range), p=2e-30. Negative association preserved.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Mean `pfs_months` differs across the five strata of `feature_064` (race: white/black/hispanic/asian/other), with a meaningful PFS deficit in non-white groups.
Mean `pfs_months` differs across the four strata of `feature_018` (insurance: private/medicare/medicaid/uninsured), with uninsured patients having shorter PFS.

analyses.result_summary: Group mean PFS: white 4.316, asian 4.306, black 4.273, hispanic 4.315, other 4.407. Spread is ~0.13 months, all within noise. Overall ANOVA p=0.479. NO race-based PFS disparity.

Group mean PFS: private 4.330, medicare 4.312, medicaid 4.277, uninsured 4.249. Uninsured-vs-private contrast = -0.082 mo, p=0.13. Overall ANOVA p=0.219. NO insurance-based PFS disparity at the unadjusted level.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A single multivariable linear model with `feature_078`, `feature_057` (categorical), `feature_051`, `feature_038`, `feature_013`, `feature_043`, `feature_109`, `feature_067`, `feature_099`, `feature_092`, `feature_009` will explain a large share of variance in `pfs_months` (R^2 > 0.5).
Each of the top eleven candidate predictors retains a significant independent contribution after mutual adjustment (i.e., none of the strong univariate effects is fully confounded by the others).

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + feature_051 + feature_038 + feature_013 + feature_043 + feature_109 + feature_067 + feature_099 + feature_092 + feature_009', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: R^2 = 0.858 (n=50,000). Adjusted coefficients (all p<<0.001 except feature_067): feature_078 +0.176/yr; feature_057[T.1] -1.196, [T.2] -2.329; feature_051 -1.361; feature_038 +0.943; feature_013 -0.353; feature_043 -0.287; feature_109 -0.356; feature_067 +0.021 (p=0.37, NS); feature_099 -0.076; feature_092 +0.469; feature_009 -0.0050. All ten besides feature_067 retain large, independent, highly significant contributions; feature_067 is fully attenuated by the others.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among binary features with mid-range prevalence (5%-50%), at least some pairs are mutually exclusive or strongly negatively correlated, which would suggest competing treatment regimens or one-of-many encoding.

analyses.result_summary: Among the 25 highest-prevalence (excluding very rare) binary features, NO pair achieves Pearson r < -0.1. The features behave as independent indicators rather than mutually-exclusive regimen flags. Hypothesis NOT supported: there is no obvious competing-treatment encoding within the binary feature set.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The prevalence of `feature_038` (the apparent beneficial treatment) is meaningfully different across `feature_057` strata — for example, it is given preferentially to lower-severity (feature_057=0) patients, which could partly explain its raw univariate benefit.
The prevalence of `feature_051` (poor-prognostic) is meaningfully different across `feature_057` strata.

analyses.result_summary: feature_038 prevalence by feature_057: level 0 → 0.205, level 1 → 0.198, level 2 → 0.197. Differences are ~0.7 percentage points — feature_038 is allocated essentially uniformly across feature_057. Hypothesis NOT supported.
feature_051 prevalence by feature_057: 0.552 / 0.548 / 0.551. Effectively identical (<1 percentage-point spread). Hypothesis NOT supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a statistically significant interaction between `feature_038` and `feature_013` on `pfs_months`: the PFS benefit of feature_038 is smaller (or absent) when feature_013=1.
There is a statistically significant interaction between `feature_038` and `feature_043` on `pfs_months`: the PFS benefit of feature_038 is smaller when feature_043=1.
There is a positive interaction between `feature_038` and `feature_067` on `pfs_months`: the PFS benefit of feature_038 is greater when feature_067=1.
There is no `feature_038` × `feature_051` interaction on `pfs_months` despite both having strong main effects.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_038 * feature_013 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_038:feature_013 = -1.640 mo, p<1e-300. Strong negative interaction: in feature_013=0 patients the feature_038 main effect is roughly +1.5–1.8 mo, but in feature_013=1 patients it is essentially abolished. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_038 * feature_043 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_038:feature_043 = -1.450 mo, p<1e-300. Same pattern: feature_038 benefit is large in feature_043=0 but largely abolished when feature_043=1. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_038 * feature_067 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_038:feature_067 = +0.892 mo, p=1.1e-31. Significant positive synergy — when feature_067=1, the PFS benefit of feature_038 is roughly doubled. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_038 * feature_051 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_038:feature_051 = +0.0287 mo, p=0.189. NOT significant; effects are additive. Hypothesis SUPPORTED (predicted no interaction).

proposed_hypotheses.text: The PFS benefit of `feature_038` is consistent across `feature_057` strata (no interaction): treatment benefit is roughly the same magnitude in low-, mid-, and high-severity patients.

analyses.result_summary: Stratified mean PFS gain from feature_038: feature_057=0 → +0.92 mo (5.08 → 6.01, p=5e-76, n=3613/13979); feature_057=1 → +0.99 mo (3.86 → 4.85, p=4e-122, n=4945/20026); feature_057=2 → +0.90 mo (2.73 → 3.63, p=3e-34, n=1464/5973). Benefit is essentially identical across strata — supports no interaction with feature_057. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a statistically significant interaction between `feature_078` (age) and `feature_038` on `pfs_months`: treatment benefit varies with age.

analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_078:feature_038 = +0.00033 mo per year, p=0.801. NOT significant; benefit of feature_038 is approximately constant across age. Hypothesis NOT supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for `feature_078`, `feature_057`, and the major prognostic biomarkers (`feature_051`, `feature_038`, `feature_013`, `feature_043`, `feature_099`, `feature_092`), uninsured patients (`feature_018`='uninsured') still have meaningfully lower `pfs_months` than privately-insured patients.
After the same adjustment, race (`feature_064`) still shows a meaningful PFS gradient (e.g., black or hispanic patients lower than white).

analyses.code: smf.ols("pfs_months ~ C(feature_018, Treatment(reference='private')) + feature_078 + C(feature_057) + feature_051 + feature_038 + feature_013 + feature_043 + feature_099 + feature_092", data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted insurance contrasts (vs private): medicaid +0.0039 (p=0.75), medicare -0.0029 (p=0.74), uninsured -0.024 (p=0.23). Joint F-test of insurance after adjustment p=0.33. NO insurance disparity even after rich clinical adjustment.

analyses.code: smf.ols("pfs_months ~ C(feature_064, Treatment(reference='white')) + feature_078 + C(feature_057) + feature_051 + feature_038", data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted race contrasts (vs white): asian +0.006, black +0.011, hispanic -0.003, other +0.038. Joint F-test p=0.69. NO race disparity even after adjustment. All point estimates are within ±0.04 mo of white.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the other ordinal multi-level integer features (`feature_025`, `feature_075`, `feature_071`, `feature_026`, `feature_096`, `feature_033`), at least one shows a significant ANOVA association with `pfs_months`.

analyses.result_summary: ANOVA p-values: feature_025 p=0.047 (just under 0.05; mean range 4.23-4.36, no monotonic trend); feature_075 p=0.64; feature_071 (11 levels) p=0.20; feature_026 p=0.83; feature_096 p=0.069; feature_033 p=0.13. Only feature_025 reaches conventional significance, and the effect is ~0.13 months — not clinically meaningful. Hypothesis WEAKLY supported (one feature at p<0.05) but with negligible magnitude.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patients with `feature_057`=2 have substantially higher odds of immediate progression (`pfs_months`==0) than patients with `feature_057`=0.
`feature_038`=1 protects against immediate progression (`pfs_months`==0) after adjusting for other prognostic factors.
`feature_051`=1 increases odds of immediate progression (`pfs_months`==0).
Older `feature_078` (age) decreases odds of immediate progression (`pfs_months`==0) after adjusting for stage and treatment.

analyses.code: smf.logit('pfs0 ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + feature_051 + feature_038 + feature_013 + feature_043', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: PFS=0 occurs in 1,403 of 50,000 patients (2.81%). Logistic regression: feature_057[T.1] log-OR = +4.52 (p=3e-94), feature_057[T.2] log-OR = +9.14 (p=4e-200) — extreme increase in immediate-progression risk for higher feature_057. feature_051 log-OR = +5.38 (p=1e-163). feature_038 log-OR = -0.88 (p=6e-10). feature_078 log-OR per year = -0.673 (p=4e-286) — older age is strongly protective against immediate progression. All four hypotheses SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Log-transforming the right-skewed continuous predictors `feature_099` and `feature_009` will produce a stronger and more linear association with `pfs_months` than the raw scale.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + log_f009 + log_f099 + feature_092', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: After log1p transform of feature_009 and feature_099 (and adjusting for age and feature_057): log_f009 slope = -0.148 mo per log-unit (p=2e-151); log_f099 slope = -0.295 mo per log-unit (p<1e-300); feature_092 slope = +0.453 (p<1e-300). Both log slopes are large in standardized terms and clearly more interpretable than raw-scale slopes (raw f099 slope was -0.075). Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a significant `feature_038` × `feature_064` (race) interaction on `pfs_months`: the magnitude of the apparent treatment benefit of feature_038 differs by race group.

analyses.code: smf.ols("pfs_months ~ C(feature_064, Treatment(reference='white')) * feature_038 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)", data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Joint F-test for the four feature_064:feature_038 interaction terms: p=0.076 (NS at 0.05). Individual interaction coefs vs white reference: asian +0.056 (p=0.31), black +0.089 (p=0.031), hispanic +0.033 (p=0.39), other +0.189 (p=0.052). Only the black-vs-white contrast crosses 0.05 individually, and the joint test is null. No robust evidence of race-based treatment heterogeneity. Hypothesis NOT supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a significant `feature_038` × `feature_018` (insurance) interaction on `pfs_months`: the magnitude of treatment benefit differs by insurance type.

analyses.code: smf.ols("pfs_months ~ C(feature_018, Treatment(reference='private')) * feature_038 + feature_078 + C(feature_057)", data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Joint F-test for the three feature_018:feature_038 interaction terms: p=0.130 (NS). Individual coefs vs private: medicaid +0.050 (p=0.22), medicare -0.036 (p=0.21), uninsured -0.074 (p=0.30). No insurance-based treatment heterogeneity. Hypothesis NOT supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Adding additional continuous candidate predictors (`feature_006`, `feature_055`, `feature_070`, `feature_028`, `feature_065`) to the multivariable model meaningfully improves R^2 beyond the eleven-predictor model.
Standardized coefficients in the expanded model show that `feature_078` (age, z-scored) is by far the largest single contributor to PFS variance.

analyses.result_summary: Expanded model R^2 = 0.8586 versus core model R^2 = 0.8584. ΔR^2 ≈ 0.0002 — negligible improvement. Among the new variables only feature_006 retains p<<0.05 (raw coef -4e-4, p=5e-19); feature_055 (p=0.057), feature_070 (p=0.74), feature_028 (p=0.68), feature_065 (p=0.88) are not meaningful contributors. Hypothesis NOT supported (no meaningful R^2 gain).

Standardized (per-SD) coefficients on PFS: feature_078_z +1.750 mo (huge — one SD ≈ 10 yrs of age); feature_051 -1.362; feature_057[T.2] -2.329; feature_057[T.1] -1.196; feature_038 +0.943; feature_109 -0.357; feature_013 -0.354; feature_099_z -0.294; feature_043 -0.286; feature_092_z +0.235; feature_009_z -0.081. feature_078 (age) has the largest single per-SD contribution, ~30% larger than feature_057[T.2]. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_038`=1 sharply increases the odds of long PFS (`pfs_months` > 10 months) after adjusting for `feature_078`, `feature_057`, `feature_051`, `feature_013`, `feature_043`, `feature_099`, and `feature_092`.
`feature_013`=1 and `feature_043`=1 are individually associated with much lower odds of long PFS (>10 mo).

analyses.code: smf.logit('long_pfs ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + feature_051 + feature_038 + feature_013 + feature_043 + feature_099 + feature_092', data=df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Long PFS (>10 mo) occurs in 766 of 50,000 patients (1.53%). Logistic regression: feature_038 log-OR = +4.63 (OR≈103, p=7e-236) — the dominant predictor of long PFS. feature_013 log-OR = -3.39 (OR≈0.034, p=2e-87); feature_043 log-OR = -3.36 (OR≈0.035, p=7e-68). feature_078 log-OR = +0.204/yr (p<1e-200); feature_051 log-OR = -1.23 (p=3e-36); feature_057[T.2] log-OR = -2.58 (p=2e-32); feature_099 log-OR = -0.095/unit (p=7e-13); feature_092 log-OR = +0.565/unit (p=4e-9). Both hypotheses SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a significant three-way `feature_038` × `feature_057` × `feature_078` interaction on `pfs_months`: treatment benefit depends on age conditional on stage.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_038 * f057_high * feature_078', data=df).fit()  # f057_high = (feature_057>=1).astype(int)
analyses.result_summary: Three-way interaction coefficient feature_038 : f057_high : feature_078 = +0.00261, p=0.368 (NS). The two-way feature_038:feature_078 interaction is also NS (p=0.57) and feature_038:f057_high is NS (p=0.23). No evidence of three-way modulation. Hypothesis NOT supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among binary features with prevalence near 50% (`feature_088` prev=0.45, `feature_005` 0.45, `feature_012` 0.48, `feature_106` 0.55) — features that *might* encode sex or another roughly balanced demographic — none has a significant association with `pfs_months`, consistent with these being demographic rather than prognostic markers.

analyses.code: for c in sex_cands: stats.ttest_ind(g1, g0)
analyses.result_summary: PFS difference (1 vs 0) and p-values: feature_088 +0.019 (p=0.36); feature_005 -0.004 (p=0.86); feature_012 +0.012 (p=0.56); feature_106 -0.015 (p=0.46). All four are statistically NULL — consistent with neutral demographic/structural variables. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_071` (11 ordered levels 0-10, prevalence-weighted, count distribution decreasing with level) has a linear monotonic relationship with `pfs_months`.

analyses.result_summary: Pearson r = -0.0027, p=0.55. Mean PFS by level: 4.32, 4.32, 4.30, 4.27, 4.30, 4.40, 4.29, 4.28, 4.36, 4.29, 4.19 — non-monotonic, total range ~0.21 mo. Hypothesis NOT supported; feature_071 is not a useful PFS predictor.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Other features in the dataset are strongly correlated with `feature_057`, suggesting that feature_057 reflects a clinical state observable through several proxies (e.g., common laboratory or comorbidity profile).

analyses.result_summary: Excluding the derived f057_high indicator (r=0.87 by construction) and PFS-derived flags (pfs0 r=0.16; long_pfs r=-0.06), the strongest external correlations with feature_057 are: feature_004 r=-0.014 (p=0.002), feature_043 r=+0.011 (p=0.018), feature_107 r=+0.010 (p=0.025), feature_061 r=+0.009 (p=0.046). All |r| < 0.02 — feature_057 is essentially uncorrelated with every other feature in the dataset. Hypothesis NOT supported: feature_057 is an isolated, independently distributed prognostic axis rather than a syndromic summary of other observed variables.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The receipt rate of `feature_038` (the apparent beneficial treatment) differs across `feature_064` (race) groups.
The receipt rate of `feature_038` differs across `feature_018` (insurance) groups.
The prevalence of the poor-prognostic `feature_051` differs across race or insurance groups (which would have been a confounding pathway for any disparity).

analyses.result_summary: feature_038 receipt rate by race: white 0.200, asian 0.211, black 0.201, hispanic 0.198, other 0.192. Spread = 1.9 percentage points. Chi-square test (race × feature_038) on 50,000 records: p≈0.4 (effectively null). Hypothesis NOT supported — treatment allocation is essentially equal across race groups in this dataset.

feature_038 receipt rate by insurance: private 0.201, medicare 0.202, medicaid 0.198, uninsured 0.190. Spread = 1.2 percentage points. Hypothesis NOT supported — no meaningful insurance gradient in receipt of feature_038.

feature_051 prevalence by race: 0.547-0.558 across all groups (range 1 percentage point). By insurance: 0.538-0.556 (range 1.8 points). No meaningful gradient. Hypothesis NOT supported. Combined with the null treatment-allocation findings, this confirms that there is no observed disparity pathway in this anonymized cohort: race and insurance are independent of both clinical state and treatment receipt, and the adjusted PFS contrasts are flat.
